items.q: What condition must be met for total internal reflection to occur?
items.correct: Light must travel from a denser to a less dense medium AND strike the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle.
items.misconception: Any angle is fine as long as the light is going into a less dense medium.
items.random_wrong: Light must go from a less dense to a denser medium.
items.why: Students remember 'denser to less dense' but forget the angle condition, or forget the direction requirement entirely.

items.q: Why does light bend when it enters a denser medium like glass?
items.correct: Because it slows down — the change in speed at the boundary causes the wavefront to bend (towards the normal).
items.misconception: Because the medium is denser, it physically blocks part of the light and forces it to turn.
items.random_wrong: Because gravity in the glass pulls the light down.
items.why: Students attach refraction to 'density' as a material property without linking it to the change in wave speed and Snell's law.

items.q: Is the internal energy of a system the same as the heat it contains?
items.correct: No — a system doesn't 'contain heat'; internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of its particles, changed by heating or doing work.
items.misconception: Yes — internal energy is just the stored heat in the object.
items.random_wrong: No — internal energy refers only to the chemical potential energy.
items.why: Students treat heat as a substance stored in objects rather than a process of energy transfer, blurring the heat/internal energy distinction.

items.q: Refractive index of a medium is:
items.why: It's c / v. “v / c” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Total internal reflection requires light travelling:
items.why: It's Denser to less dense. “Less dense to denser” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Glass n = 1.5. Critical angle for glass-air boundary? (sin⁻¹(1/1.5) ≈ 41.8°)
items.why: Exact answer: 42. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
